meta.name: Cascadia Wild-Edible Plant ID Dataset
meta.bioregion: Pacific Northwest / Cascadia — Washington, Oregon, Idaho, southern British Columbia
meta.purpose: Grounding data for an educational plant-ID feature.
meta.disclaimer: EDUCATIONAL FIELD-ID AID ONLY. This data must never be used to tell a user a wild plant is safe to eat. The eat/don't-eat decision always stays with the user and a qualified field guide. Below a confidence threshold, suppress the species name entirely.
safety_notes: Lethal-dose numbers are deliberately qualitative ('a small amount can be fatal'); specific thresholds in the literature are low/moderate confidence.

Do not conflate the two hemlocks: Conium (poison hemlock) = hairless purple-blotched stem, paralysis; Cicuta (water hemlock) = chambered yellow-oily root, seizures. Purple stem mottling belongs to Conium, NOT Cicuta.

Elderberry vs hemlock rule: elderberry is a WOODY shrub with bark, OPPOSITE leaves, and true berries; hemlocks are herbaceous (no bark) with ALTERNATE finely-divided leaves and dry seeds, never berries.
No plant bearing a true raspberry/blackberry-form aggregate drupelet fruit is dangerously poisonous in North America — but still teach the fruit form so users don't over-generalize.
Idaho range is LOW confidence for the wet-westside species (salmonberry, red huckleberry, salal); they are chiefly coastal / west of the Cascade crest.

Red baneberry is the #1 deadly lookalike for nearly every red/dark forest berry (huckleberries, bearberry, bunchberry, soapberry, highbush cranberry) and its berries may be RED or WHITE — ID by GROWTH FORM (soft herb with compound toothed leaves + dark-eyed raceme vs woody shrub/mat), never by color.

Highest-stakes confusion is common camas vs death camas: identical when not flowering, intermixed in the same meadows, and cooking does NOT detoxify a misidentified death-camas bulb. Flower color (blue vs cream/white) is the only reliable field separator — harvest only in bloom.

Carrot-family (Apiaceae) caution: watercress and cow parsnip share habitat with deadly water hemlock and poison hemlock. Never present any wild umbel-flowered carrot-family plant as edible without expert hands-on confirmation.
Cattail vs yellow flag iris: the toxic iris rhizome is exactly the part most likely mistaken for the edible cattail rhizome — never ID cattail by leaves alone and never taste-test to ID.

Several species are kept as edibility 'caution' because preparation or a deadly lookalike makes casual foraging unsafe (camas, cattail, wapato, soapberry, kinnikinnick, sheep sorrel, redwood sorrel, cow parsnip, Cascade Oregon grape).
Excluded: 'youth-on-age' (Tolmiea menziesii) was dropped from the edible set — its only food record is a single bitter tribal account; not a credibly documented edible.
sources_general: Pojar & MacKinnon, Plants of the Pacific Northwest Coast
Burke Herbarium Image Collection (University of Washington)
USDA Forest Service FEIS / USDA PLANTS
WSU & OSU Extension; Washington Native Plant Society (WNPS)
WSU, Selected Poisonous Plants of the Pacific Northwest: https://wpcdn.web.wsu.edu/extension/uploads/sites/54/2019/05/SelectedPoisonousPlantsOfThePacificNW_Kerr-PoisonousPlants1007-2005.pdf
meta.verification_note: Several WSU/OSU pages block automated fetch (HTTP 403); those facts were cross-corroborated via Burke, USDA/FEIS, WNPS, NPS, CDC, NCBI/PMC, Cornell. A human should open the WSU/OSU/BLM source pages directly to confirm exact wording before this data is relied on in production.

species.edible_part: Ripe aggregate berry (orange to red to dark reddish-purple, raspberry-form); young spring shoots, peeled.
species.preparation: Berries raw OK. Shoots peeled, eaten raw or steamed before they turn woody.
species.season: Berries early May to late July (earliest-fruiting PNW Rubus); shoots in spring.
species.habitat: Moist coastal forests, streambanks, seeps, wetland edges; dense thickets; wet shade.
species.range: Strong on the wet westside (W. WA, W. OR, coastal/S. BC). Uncommon/local in Idaho (edge of range).
species.safety_caveat: No deadly lookalike — orange raspberry-form berry with magenta flowers and 3 leaflets is nearly unique. Avoid sprayed/roadside plants. Flavor varies plant-to-plant; that is normal, not toxicity.

species.edible_part: Ripe bright-red berry only.
species.preparation: Raw OK (tart); also jam/syrup. No cooking needed.
species.season: Mid–late summer (~July–September).
species.habitat: Conifer-forest understory; characteristically grows from rotting stumps / nurse logs; acidic humus.
species.range: Common west of the Cascade crest (WA, OR, SW/coastal BC). Sparse/largely absent in arid interior Idaho — range confidence LOW for ID.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Red berry color.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Baneberry is a soft herb with large toothed compound leaves and glossy berries in ONE upright terminal cluster, each berry with a dark 'eye' (stigma scar). Huckleberry is a woody twiggy shrub with tiny simple leaves and berries hanging singly from leaf axils, with no eye.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Red berries on a shrub.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Elderberry has large pinnately compound leaves and dense cone-shaped clusters of hundreds of tiny berries; huckleberry has small simple leaves and single hanging berries.

species.safety_caveat: Safe-ID rule: red huckleberries hang SINGLY from a woody, green angular-stemmed shrub with small simple leaves. Berries in an upright cluster on a herbaceous plant = baneberry — do not eat.

species.edible_part: Ripe dull blue-black berry (fleshy calyx, slightly hairy/bumpy surface); young leaves as mild tea.
species.preparation: Raw OK (sweet, mealy, seedy); also dried cakes, jam, syrup.
species.season: Late summer into fall (~July–September, persisting).
species.habitat: Dominant evergreen understory in moist-to-dry conifer forest; peatlands, dunes, coastal bluffs; salt-tolerant.
species.range: Very common west of the Cascades (WA, OR, coastal/SW BC). Largely absent interior/eastern Idaho — range confidence LOW for ID.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Dark berries in disturbed/edge habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Nightshade is a soft sprawling/climbing vine with purple star flowers and smooth glossy berries; salal is a woody evergreen shrub with leathery toothed leaves and a HAIRY berry on a hairy reddish stalk.

species.safety_caveat: No close deadly lookalike in Cascadia. Confirm a woody evergreen leathery-leaved shrub with a hairy berry on a hairy stalk to rule out a smooth-skinned nightshade.

species.edible_part: Ripe dusty blue-black berries in grape-like clusters. (Yellow roots/inner bark are berberine-rich — medicinal, NOT food.)
species.preparation: Berries raw OK but intensely sour/seedy — usually cooked into jelly/syrup or mixed with sweeter berries (traditionally salal). Roots are not a food (GI upset; contraindicated in pregnancy and with some medications).
species.season: Yellow flowers early spring; berries late summer–fall (sweeter after frost).
species.habitat: Evergreen shrub of open forests, edges, rocky slopes, clearings; sun to part shade.
species.range: Native and widespread across WA, OR, ID, and into S. BC — one of the few here genuinely occurring in interior/eastern ID and east of the Cascades. Confidence HIGH WA/OR, MODERATE-HIGH ID/BC.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Both in edge/disturbed habitat; purple flowers and green berries confuse novices.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Nightshade is a soft sprawling/climbing vine with purple star flowers and smooth glossy berries; Oregon grape is a stiff woody shrub with glossy spiny holly-like compound leaves and dusty-blue berries in grape clusters.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Dark berries; present but uncommon in Cascadia (naturalized in disturbed ground, esp. Seattle area). Frequency confidence MODERATE.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Belladonna is a soft herb with solitary purple-brown bell flowers and a single shiny black berry seated in a green 5-pointed star calyx — no spiny holly leaves, no grape clusters.

species.safety_caveat: One rule separates Oregon grape from all toxic dark berries: it is a woody shrub with stiff glossy spine-toothed HOLLY-LIKE compound leaves and dusty-blue berries in grape-like clusters. Do not confuse culinary berry use with the medicinal berberine root.

species.edible_part: Ripe soft red dome/thimble-shaped aggregate berry (hollow when picked); young shoots peeled.
species.preparation: Raw OK (very soft, seedy — eat fresh, dried, or as jam).
species.season: White flowers April–July; berries ~July–September.
species.habitat: Forest openings/edges, thickets, meadows, clearings, riparian; sea level to subalpine; tolerates more sun and drier sites than salmonberry.
species.range: Widespread and common in all four areas — both sides of the Cascades, throughout OR, common in ID (esp. northern/mountain forests), and BC.
species.safety_caveat: Arguably the safest in this set. Diagnostic: THORNLESS stems (unique among PNW Rubus) plus large soft fuzzy maple-shaped (5-lobed palmate) leaves. Teach thornless + maple-leaf + hollow-red-cap fruit together so beginners don't over-trust 'fuzzy maple leaf' alone.

species.edible_part: RIPE BERRIES ONLY, and only after cooking AND straining out the seeds. Seeds, stems, bark, leaves, roots, flowers, and unripe/green berries are all toxic.
species.preparation: MANDATORY: (1) fully ripe, (2) cooked, AND (3) seed-strained. Raw red berries are bitter, nausea-inducing, considered poisonous. More contentious than blue elder — multiple sources (WNPS, USDA plant guide) state the red fruit 'should not be eaten' and may carry higher toxin levels. For a safety-critical app: never present as raw-edible; when in doubt, treat as toxic.
species.toxicity: Cyanogenic glycosides (sambunigrin, prunasin) release hydrogen cyanide in the gut. Symptoms: nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dizziness, weakness; severe cases cyanosis, confusion, hypotension. Cooking substantially reduces glycosides.
species.season: Flowers April–May; bright-red CONE/pyramidal (not flat-topped) berry clusters ripen ~June–August.
species.habitat: Moist forests, edges, streambanks, clearings, roadsides; very common.
species.range: Most common elderberry on the NW coast; abundant throughout WA/OR/ID/S. BC, both sides of the Cascades.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Foragers handling roots/stems of wetland plants; hemlock has white flower umbels.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Elderberry is a WOODY shrub with bark, OPPOSITE leaves, and true berries; water hemlock is herbaceous with ALTERNATE finely-divided leaves, white umbels, dry seeds (no berries), and a chambered root. See toxic_reference.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: White-umbel herb in the same disturbed/streambank habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Elderberry is woody with berries; poison hemlock is a herb with a hairless purple-blotched stem and dry seeds. See toxic_reference.

species.safety_caveat: NEVER raw. Gate on ripe + cooked + seeds removed. All vegetative parts and unripe berries are poisonous; treat anyone digging roots or using stems as a poisoning risk.

species.edible_part: RIPE cooked berries (blue with powdery white bloom); flowers (elderflower). NOT raw berries, NOT stems/leaves/bark/roots, NOT unripe (green/red-stage) berries.
species.preparation: Cook ripe berries (jam, syrup, pie, wine). Strip berries off stems — never press leaves/stems into juice. High-acid fruit; cans with standard high-acid recipes (OSU). The more palatable / less-contentious of the two PNW species, but the cook-before-eating rule still applies.
species.toxicity: Same cyanogenic-glycoside class. OSU: all parts contain cyanogenic glycosides; cooking the berries/juice eliminates the risk. Roots highest; leaves/stems/bark/seeds/unripe berries toxic. Note: in the 1983 CDC raw-juice illness case, acute cyanide was not confirmed — illness was attributed to including leaves/stems in raw juice. Safe framing: don't press leaves/stems and cook the juice.
species.season: Flat-topped white flower umbels late spring–summer; powdery-blue berries ripen late summer–fall (Aug–Oct).
species.habitat: Open sites, edges, streamsides, roadsides, dry-to-moist slopes/valleys.
species.range: Widespread WA/OR/ID + S. BC; more common east of the Cascades / in drier interior than red elder, but present throughout.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: White flower umbels in wet habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Elderberry is woody with bark, opposite leaves, true berries; water hemlock is herbaceous with alternate divided leaves and a chambered root. See toxic_reference.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: White-umbel herb in disturbed habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Poison hemlock has a hairless purple-blotched stem and dry seeds, not berries on a woody shrub. See toxic_reference.

species.safety_caveat: Cook ripe berries; never eat raw stems/leaves/unripe berries; never press leaves/stems into juice. Use flowers only, not green stems.

species.edible_part: Young tender spring leaves / growing tips (best ~4–10 in tall, before flowering). Avoid mature/flowering plants (gritty cystoliths).
species.preparation: CRITICAL: must be cooked, dried, or thoroughly crushed before eating. Heat (blanch 30–60 s / steam / boil) or full drying neutralizes the stinging hairs (silica trichomes injecting histamine, acetylcholine, serotonin, formic/oxalic acids). Handle with gloves until cooked. For a safety app: cook or dry — do not eat raw.
species.season: Tender window ~Feb–April/May; flowers May–Sept (stop eating leaves after).
species.habitat: Moist rich soils — shady lowlands, streambanks, riparian thickets, ditches, disturbed ground, forest edges; sea level to subalpine; dense rhizomatous colonies.
species.range: Widespread both sides of the Cascades and throughout OR, ID, S. BC.
benign_lookalikes: Hedge-nettles/mints (Stachys) — square stems, don't sting, showy lipped flowers
species.safety_caveat: No deadly lookalikes — the sting is a near-foolproof self-ID. Always cook/dry; gloves; young pre-flower growth only; avoid roadside/sprayed plants (bioaccumulates). People on blood thinners/BP/diuretic meds should consult a clinician.

species.edible_part: Entire above-ground plant — leaves (including the perfoliate disk leaf), stems, flowers.
species.preparation: Edible raw (salads); can be lightly steamed. No cooking needed for safety.
species.season: Cool-season annual; best late winter–spring (~Feb–May); flowers ~May–July, then toughens/reddens.
species.habitat: Moist, shady/cool sites — open to shaded forest, thickets, meadows, streambanks, disturbed ground; many substrates including rock crevices/scree.
species.range: Native and common throughout Cascadia, both sides of the Cascades; BC south through OR to CA, east to ID/Rockies.
species.safety_caveat: No relevant toxic lookalike in Cascadia — the mature PERFOLIATE disk leaf (round cup-like leaf with the flower stalk emerging through its center) is essentially unique. Don't ID from the juvenile linear-leaf rosette; wait for the perfoliate leaf + central flower stalk. Contains soluble oxalates (usually low, can accumulate in N-rich/fertilized soils) — eat in moderation; people with kidney stones/disease should limit; wash well and avoid runoff/roadside sites.

species.edible_part: Ripe shiny-black aggregate berry (red unripe). Note: dioecious — only female plants fruit.
species.preparation: Raw OK; fresh, baked, jam. Widely considered the best-flavored PNW blackberry.
species.season: White-pink flowers spring; fruit mid–late summer (~July–August).
species.habitat: Forest edges, clearings, clearcuts, open woods, roadsides, disturbed ground; low trailing/scrambling vine.
species.range: Native BC to CA, coast inland to the Cascades and into central ID. The only blackberry native to BC; present in all four areas.
benign_lookalikes: Himalayan blackberry (Rubus armeniacus, syn. R. bifrons / R. discolor / R. procerus) — invasive but edible; massive arching shrub, stout ridged thorny canes, ~5 leaflets with pale undersides
Cutleaf/evergreen blackberry (Rubus laciniatus) — invasive but edible; deeply dissected leaflets
Black raspberry (Rubus leucodermis) — edible; glaucous whitish-blue canes, hollow-cored fruit
species.safety_caveat: Native-vs-invasive is an ecology/quality question, not safety — all are edible. No aggregate-drupelet (raspberry/blackberry-form) fruit in North America is dangerously poisonous. Avoid roadside/sprayed plants (invasives are often sprayed); watch fine thorns. Fruitless plants are normal (males don't fruit).

species.edible_part: Ripe purplish/bluish-black berry (4–7 mm), in short axillary racemes.
species.preparation: Raw OK; also cooked, dried, frozen.
species.season: Fruit ~July–October (one of the last PNW huckleberries to ripen; often best after frost).
species.habitat: Low-elevation conifer forest, edges, dunes, coastal salt-spray; evergreen leathery-leaved shrub 0.5–3 m.
species.range: W. WA, W. OR (Coast Range), coastal S. BC. Idaho: ABSENT (high confidence) — a reported ID 'evergreen huckleberry' is a misID.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Glossy clustered forest berries (red OR white).
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Baneberry is a soft herb with large compound toothed leaves and berries in one upright raceme, each with a dark eye; this is a woody evergreen shrub with racemose berries and no eye.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Shiny black forest berries.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Twinberry has OPPOSITE leaves and black berries in pairs cradled by reddish-purple bracts; huckleberry leaves alternate.

species.safety_caveat: Deadly baneberry shares this understory; ID by growth form (woody evergreen shrub), never by berry color. Only PNW Vaccinium with leathery evergreen leaves + racemose flowers.

species.edible_part: Ripe dark purple/black berry (7–9 mm), non-glaucous/shiny, borne SINGLY in leaf axils.
species.preparation: Raw OK; jams/syrups; traditionally dried into cakes.
species.season: Fruit mid-summer through late August/fall.
species.habitat: Dry-to-moist conifer forest, openings, avalanche chutes; mid/high elevation (most abundant ~4,000–6,000 ft); deciduous shrub with shredding gray bark.
species.range: WA (both sides), OR Cascades, ID (HIGH — Idaho state fruit), S. BC.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Shares moist forest; berries red OR white.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Huckleberry has woody shredding-bark stems and single axillary dark non-glaucous berries with no eye; baneberry is a soft herb with compound toothed leaves and a raceme of dark-eyed berries.

species.safety_caveat: Reject any berries in a cluster/raceme on a soft non-woody stem, any berry with a black eye, or any red/white glossy berries (baneberry).

synonyms: Vaccinium alaskaense (treated as part of a hybridizing complex)
species.edible_part: Ripe bluish/purplish-black berry with whitish bloom, solitary in leaf axils; crown-shaped calyx scar.
species.preparation: Raw commonly acceptable (tart); also cooked/dried.
species.season: Fruit ~mid-July through August/September.
species.habitat: Cool moist conifer forest, bog/meadow edges; acidic soil; submontane to subalpine; strongly ANGLED twigs.
species.range: WA (both sides + Olympics), OR Cascades, S. BC. Idaho: panhandle, MODERATE confidence (peripheral).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Shared moist shaded forest.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Woody shrub with small simple ENTIRE oval leaves + solitary blue-black berry with crown calyx scar, vs herb with compound toothed leaves + erect raceme of red/dark-eyed berries.

species.safety_caveat: Decisive checks = growth form, leaf margin (entire vs toothed/compound), fruit arrangement. Never key on color. (V. ovalifolium / V. alaskaense taxonomy is unsettled — treat as a complex.)

species.edible_part: Blue glaucous berries only. Yellow roots/inner bark are berberine-rich — medicinal/dye, NOT food.
species.preparation: Berries raw possible but very tart; standard = cooked + sweetened (jelly/wine; high pectin, often combined with salal).
species.season: Berries ripen ~July–August, persisting into fall.
species.habitat: Understory of submontane-montane conifer/mixed forest; pinnately compound spiny holly-like leaves (9–21 leaflets).
species.range: Chiefly W. WA + coast, W. Cascades & NE OR, S. BC. Idaho: rare disjunct panhandle only (LOW-MODERATE).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Spiny glossy holly-like leaves; invasive in same forests.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Oregon grape leaves are pinnately COMPOUND (each leaflet one central vein); holly leaves are SIMPLE/net-veined with bright RED berries.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Dark blue-black berries of similar color.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Buckthorn berries grow directly off twigs on simple-leaved shrubs, not in upright clusters on spiny compound foliage.

species.safety_caveat: Berberine concentrated in yellow roots/inner bark — not casual food; avoid in pregnancy and for jaundiced infants. Confirm compound spiny leaflets + blue glaucous berry clusters; rule out red-berried holly and blue-berried buckthorn.

species.edible_part: Ripe dark purple pome (~10–14 mm), often glaucous. Eat ripe flesh; do NOT crush or eat large quantities of seeds.
species.preparation: Ripe fruit raw OK; jams/dried. Flesh is low-toxicity; the hazard is crushing/grinding the cyanogenic seeds.
species.season: Fruit ~June–early August.
species.habitat: Open woods, canyons, hillsides, thickets; sea level to subalpine.
species.range: Both sides of crest in WA; throughout OR; ID (HIGH, native and common statewide); S. BC.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Dark round drupes on a native shrub.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Cascara fruit in tight clusters along stems, ripens late summer/fall; serviceberry fruit in loose terminal clusters with a persistent 5-lobed calyx scar, ripens June–July.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Dark round berry-like fruit in clusters.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Buckthorn often thorny with inconspicuous flowers; serviceberry is unarmed with showy white 5-petaled spring flowers and a persistent calyx scar.

species.safety_caveat: Eat only ripe flesh; do not eat/crush seeds or make seed extracts (seeds/leaves/bark/twigs are cyanogenic). Best discriminator from toxic buckthorns: white 5-petaled flower + persistent calyx scar + loose terminal clusters.

species.edible_part: Ripe red aggregate accessory fruit; young leaves for tea.
species.preparation: Raw OK.
species.season: Fruit late spring–summer.
species.habitat: F. vesca — moist woods, streambanks, meadows; F. virginiana — open woods to gravelly meadows.
species.range: Both species BC to CA; WA both sides; ID (HIGH, both native).
benign_lookalikes: Mock/Indian strawberry (Potentilla indica) — yellow flower, bland, NOT poisonous
Cinquefoils (Potentilla) — yellow flower, dry fruit, harmless
species.safety_caveat: No deadly confusion. Rule: 3 leaflets + WHITE 5-petaled flower + juicy sweet fragrant red fruit = true strawberry. A yellow flower means mock strawberry (bland) or cinquefoil (dry) — not poisonous, just not a strawberry.

species.edible_part: Ripe reddish-purple to black aggregate fruit (~12 mm); drupelets separate cleanly leaving a hollow cap.
species.preparation: Raw OK.
species.season: Fruit ~mid–late summer.
species.habitat: Thickets, forest edges/openings, fields, hillsides; low–mid elevation.
species.range: Both sides of crest in WA; ID (HIGH); BC to S. CA, east to Rockies.
benign_lookalikes: Other native Rubus (salmonberry, thimbleberry, trailing blackberry, red raspberry) — all edible
species.safety_caveat: Diagnostic: strongly glaucous (whitish-waxy) arching canes with hooked prickles; fruit separates leaving a hollow cap. No deadly lookalike for an aggregate raspberry-form fruit ONCE the Rubus genus is positively confirmed — never use that rule as standalone clearance for any dark berry.

species.edible_part: Ripe red aggregate fruit; drupelets separate leaving a hollow cap.
species.preparation: Raw OK.
species.season: Fruit mid–late summer.
species.habitat: Woodland to open rocky mountain slopes, clearings, streambanks, talus.
species.range: Chiefly east of crest in WA; ID (HIGH); AK to CA, east across N. America.
benign_lookalikes: Other red-fruited native Rubus — all edible
species.safety_caveat: Erect shrub with peeling papery cinnamon bark and bristly canes; confirm a true raspberry-type aggregate (hollow cap on picking); eat only ripe fruit.

species.edible_part: Ripe red-to-amber translucent berries (~4–6 mm; bitter).
species.preparation: Raw is strongly bitter/soapy (saponins); traditionally whipped with water + sweetener into frothy 'sxusem' / Indian ice cream. Quantity raw → GI upset.
species.season: Berries ripen summer (~June–August).
species.habitat: Moderately dry open-to-wooded conifer areas, openings, slopes; nitrogen-fixing pioneer; an east-side/drier plant.
species.range: Both sides of crest in WA; OR; ID (HIGH); BC.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Red berries in forest/montane habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Soapberry has simple oval leaves with rusty/silvery scurfy (brown-dotted) undersides and OPPOSITE leaves; baneberry has compound toothed leaves and dark-eyed berries in an upright raceme.

species.safety_caveat: Saponins cause foaming and GI upset (nausea/diarrhea) in quantity; bitterness is a natural deterrent. Foaming when mashed is nearly diagnostic. Not for large-quantity consumption.

species.edible_part: Red berries (7–10 mm, dry/mealy/near-flavorless). Leaves (arbutin) are a traditional tea/smoke material, NOT food.
species.preparation: Berries are dry/mealy; usually cooked or used in preserves to be palatable. Leaf preparations are medicinal/diuretic — not casual food, cautions for prolonged use/pregnancy.
species.season: Berries late summer–fall, often persisting into winter.
species.habitat: Coastal bluffs/prairies, rocky balds, dry subalpine meadows, dry forest openings; low trailing/mat-forming evergreen shrub with small leathery spoon leaves.
species.range: Circumboreal; both sides of crest in WA; OR; ID (HIGH); BC.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Bright-red berries in forest/montane habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Bearberry is a low woody evergreen MAT with small simple untoothed spoon leaves and dull mealy scattered berries (no dark eye); baneberry is an erect herb with compound toothed leaves and dark-eyed berries in a raceme.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Glossy red ovoid berries.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Nightshade is a scrambling/climbing vine with purple star flowers and drooping berry clusters in wet/disturbed sites; bearberry is a dry-habitat ground mat with urn-shaped pink-white flowers.

species.safety_caveat: Berries bland/mealy; the real hazard is misID of a red berry. Confirm a low woody evergreen mat + simple untoothed leathery leaves + no dark eye; rule out baneberry and nightshade. Leaves are not casual food.

species.edible_part: Red/orange single-seeded drupes (1–1.5 cm). Not a true cranberry.
species.preparation: Tart/acidic/astringent; cooked into jelly/sauce with the single large flat seed strained out; best after frost.
species.season: Ripens late summer–fall; persists on the shrub.
species.habitat: Moist woods/swamps, streambanks, wet thickets; OPPOSITE, shallowly 3-lobed maple-like toothed leaves.
species.range: Both sides of crest in WA; OR; ID (HIGH); BC; AK to CA.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Red forest berries.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Highbush cranberry has OPPOSITE 3-lobed maple-like leaves and single-stalked drupe clusters; baneberry has compound toothed leaves and dark-eyed berries in a raceme. Easy to separate.

benign_lookalikes: Guelder rose (V. opulus var. opulus) — more bitter, not toxic
species.safety_caveat: Edible cooked; large seed + astringency make raw eating poor. Confirm opposite lobed maple-like leaves.

species.edible_part: Bright coral-red drupes (6–8 mm) in a tight terminal bunch.
species.preparation: Raw OK but bland/mealy with small hard seeds; often better cooked. Unripe berries may cause mild stomach upset.
species.season: Berries ripen late summer–fall.
species.habitat: Moist woods, deep duff, bogs; low to subalpine; low carpet-forming perennial; WHORL of 4–6 dogwood-veined leaves.
species.range: Both sides of crest in WA; OR; BC; AK to N. CA. Idaho: MODERATE-HIGH (C. canadensis).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Both low forest plants with clusters of bright red berries.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Bunchberry has a flat whorl of 4–6 simple oval dogwood-veined leaves and a tight low button-like cluster of round drupes (no dark eye); baneberry is taller with compound toothed leaflets and berries in an elongated erect raceme, each with a dark eye.

species.safety_caveat: Bunchberry itself is bland/harmless but grows alongside DEADLY red baneberry — never harvest a red berry without confirming the whorl of simple dogwood-veined leaves and the absence of a dark eye / erect raceme.

species.edible_part: The fleshy outer rind/hypanthium of the ripe hip, and petals. NOT the inner achenes ('seeds') or the surrounding hairs.
species.preparation: Rind raw or cooked. The achenes AND irritant hairs MUST be removed (halve and scoop, or cook and strain through fine mesh). Bletting after frost improves palatability.
species.season: Hips ripen late summer through fall, persisting into winter.
species.habitat: Thickets, shorelines, forest edges, riparian (R. nutkana); drier interior/sagebrush sites (R. woodsii).
species.range: R. nutkana both sides of crest in WA, AK to CA; R. woodsii primarily east of Cascades on the Columbia Plateau. ID (HIGH for R. woodsii).
benign_lookalikes: Other roses (R. gymnocarpa, R. pisocarpa, R. canina) — edible hips, treated the same
species.safety_caveat: The hairs surrounding the achenes inside the hip are folk 'itching powder' — they irritate skin, mouth, throat, and the digestive lining if swallowed. ALL seeds and hairs must be removed before eating. Avoid hips from sprayed roadsides/ornamentals.

species.edible_part: Small fleshy pome (yellow to purplish-red, 1–1.6 cm). NOT the seeds, NOT the bark.
species.preparation: Tart/sour; eaten raw or stored to soften/sweeten over time. Do not eat seeds in quantity (cyanogenic).
species.season: Flowers Apr–May; fruit ripens late summer into fall, often persisting.
species.habitat: Moist woods, swamps, streambanks, open canyons, estuary edges; sea level to moderate elevation.
species.range: Chiefly WEST of crest; AK south through coastal BC/WA/OR to NW CA. Idaho: likely ABSENT.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Similar small dark pome, similar habitat, both Rosaceae.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Cut the fruit — crabapple has an apple-like core with several small soft seeds; hawthorn has 1 (to few) rocklike stones a knife won't cut, and bears stout straight thorns. (Hawthorn flesh is itself edible — discard the stone.)

species.safety_caveat: Like all apples, the SEEDS contain amygdalin (cyanogenic when crushed) — eat flesh, not seeds; bark also cyanogenic. Verify an apple-like multi-seeded core to rule out hawthorn's single hard stone.

species.edible_part: The nut kernel inside the hard shell. The green bristly husk (involucre) is NOT edible and is the handling hazard.
species.preparation: Kernels raw or roasted (roasting improves flavor/storage). Remove the bristly husk first — WEAR GLOVES.
species.season: Nuts mature ~August–September.
species.habitat: Mesic forest understory, edges, thickets, canyon bottoms; multi-stemmed shrub with simple doubly-toothed rounded alternate leaves.
species.range: var. californica SW BC–W. WA/OR to C. CA; var. cornuta E. WA, N. ID (HIGH), W. MT.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: A smooth brown rounded 'nut' and the 'chestnut' name.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Aesculus seeds are much larger (2–5 cm) with a large pale scar, in a thick leathery/spiny capsule on a tree with opposite palmately-compound leaves; hazelnut is a small ~1–1.5 cm nut in a green bristly tubular husk on a shrub. No Aesculus is native to WA/ID — mainly an urban/ornamental confusion.

benign_lookalikes: Cultivated filbert/hazelnut (Corylus avellana) — same edible nut
species.safety_caveat: PRIMARY hazard is handling: the green husk bristles embed in and irritate bare skin during harvest/de-husking — wear gloves. Tree-nut allergy caution applies.

species.edible_part: Young spring shoots (asparagus-like); young leaves and flowers. Old stems are tough/fibrous.
species.preparation: Young shoots raw or cooked like asparagus. Field test: a fresh shoot snaps crisply.
species.season: Shoots early spring after snowmelt (~Apr–May, elevation-dependent); flowers May–Sep. Narrow shoot window.
species.habitat: Open/disturbed sites — burns (classic post-fire colonizer), clearcuts, meadows, roadsides, streambanks; sea level to subalpine.
species.range: Circumboreal; WA both sides, OR, S. BC. Idaho: MODERATE-HIGH (principal post-fire species in N. Idaho).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Plausible reddish spring-shoot confusion in shared disturbed habitat (lookalike pairing is LOW-confidence / not well documented).
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Dogbane bleeds WHITE milky sap (fireweed has none — the most reliable test) and has opposite leaves and multiple branching stems; fireweed has alternate lance-shaped leaves on a single unbranched stalk.

species.safety_caveat: The leafless shoot stage is the hardest to ID. Before eating any red-tinged spring shoot confirm: NO milky sap, ALTERNATE leaves, single unbranched stem. Milky sap or opposite leaves = discard (possible toxic dogbane).

species.edible_part: Young basal-rosette leaves, flower heads/petals, roots, unopened buds.
species.preparation: Young leaves raw; mature leaves better boiled (for bitterness, not toxicity).
species.season: Flowers Mar–Oct (peak spring); leaves best early spring.
species.habitat: Lawns, fields, pastures, roadsides, waste ground; sea level to alpine.
species.range: Ubiquitous across WA/OR/S. BC; ID (HIGH).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Near-identical yellow head, rosette, milky sap (toxic to HORSES only; itself a human edible).
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Cat's-ear has branched solid stalks with more than one head and hairy leaves; true dandelion has a single hollow scape with ONE head and smooth leaves.

benign_lookalikes: Hawkbit, hawksbeard, sow thistle — non-deadly, mostly edible
species.safety_caveat: No deadly human lookalikes, but confirm the single-head / hollow-scape / basal-rosette signature. Avoid sprayed/roadside ground; caution with Asteraceae allergy or bile-duct obstruction.

species.edible_part: Tender leaves, stems, small white flowers. Avoid wiry mature lower stems.
species.preparation: Raw OK (salad, pesto) or lightly cooked. Saponins can cause GI upset in large quantities — moderation. Often advised to avoid in pregnancy.
species.season: Cool-season annual; flowers ~Feb–Oct, often through mild lowland winters.
species.habitat: Disturbed moist ground — gardens, fields, lawns, trail/forest edges; low sprawling stems.
species.range: WA both sides; OR; S. BC; ID (HIGH presence).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Same low sprawling habit, small opposite oval leaves, same habitat; non-flowering plants look very alike.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Pimpernel has a SQUARE stem and sessile leaves with dark glandular dots underneath and salmon/orange/scarlet (or blue) flowers; chickweed has a ROUND stem with a single line of hairs that switches sides at each node, CLEAR sap, stalked dotless lower leaves, and white star flowers.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Similar small weedy habit.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Spurge bleeds MILKY white sap (chickweed sap is clear) and has tiny greenish petalless flowers.

benign_lookalikes: Mouse-ear chickweed (Cerastium fontanum) — benign, hairy all over
species.safety_caveat: Decisive step is ruling out scarlet pimpernel: confirm a round stem with a switching hair-line, CLEAR (not milky) sap, petiolate dotless lower leaves, and white star flowers. Square stems, dark-dotted sessile leaves, milky sap, or colored flowers are hard stops.

species.edible_part: Young leaves/tender shoots/tops (~6–10 in tall), flower buds, mature seeds.
species.preparation: Cooking advised; boiling reduces oxalic acid. Avoid large raw quantities.
species.season: Greens spring–summer; flowers Jun–Sep; seeds late summer–fall.
species.habitat: Disturbed/over-fertilized ground — fields, gardens, roadsides, waste lots.
species.range: WA both sides; OR; ID (HIGH); S. BC.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Young seedlings/rosettes resemble young lamb's quarters before flowering (riskiest at seedling stage).
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Lamb's quarters has a white mealy/farinose 'flour-dusted' coating on new growth (rubs off), often a reddish stem streak, and goosefoot-toothed leaves, and NEVER makes berries; nightshade is smooth/glossy with white/violet flowers and green-to-black berries. Any berry = NOT lamb's quarters.

species.safety_caveat: #1 hazard is young plants vs toxic black nightshade — confirm the mealy coating + goosefoot teeth and never eat any plant in this group bearing berries. Also accumulates NITRATES and OXALATES — caution for kidney stones, gout, pregnancy; boiling reduces oxalate.

species.edible_part: Young leaves and tender stems; small reddish flowers as a tart garnish.
species.preparation: Raw in small amounts (lemony) or cooked. Boiling and discarding the water reduces soluble oxalate. Moderation regardless.
species.season: Leaves spring–fall (best young); flowers May–Aug.
species.habitat: Disturbed open ground — balds, prairies, pastures, roadsides, lawns; acidic poor soils.
species.range: Naturalized in all four regions; WA both sides; ID (HIGH).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Arrowhead/spade-shaped leaves echo sheep sorrel's hastate leaf; established in W. WA/OR.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Arum leaves are large (hand-sized), thick, glossy, often cream/silver-veined, on long fleshy petioles, producing a hooded spathe and orange-red berries and a burning taste; sheep sorrel leaves are small, thin, matte, plain green with two narrow backward lobes and a pleasant sour taste.

benign_lookalikes: Docks (Rumex crispus, R. obtusifolius) — edible relatives, also oxalate-bearing
species.safety_caveat: OXALATE caveat — soluble oxalates (the sour taste). Moderation; particular concern for kidney stones/disease, gout, pregnancy. The toxic lookalike Arum italicum carries burning insoluble calcium-oxalate needles and bears a hood and berries (sorrel never does).

species.edible_part: Leaves (trifoliate); flowers and stems also reported eaten.
species.preparation: Raw in small amounts (lemony) or cooked. Moderation due to soluble oxalates; cooking + calcium-rich pairing reduces uptake.
species.season: Foliage spring–fall; flowers Apr–Sep.
species.habitat: Shaded, cool, moist conifer understory (Douglas-fir, coast redwood); deep-shade groundcover (leaves fold in sun).
species.range: W. WA + Olympics + west-slope Cascades; OR Coast Range + W. Cascades; SW BC. Idaho: likely ABSENT (high confidence).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Also trifoliate ('leaves of three') west of the Cascades — a CONTACT hazard.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Poison oak is a WOODY shrub/vine with larger oak-like lobed, often glossy reddish leaflets; redwood sorrel is a low non-woody groundcover with three small heart-shaped (notched) leaflets. Woody or oak-lobed = NOT sorrel; do not touch.

benign_lookalikes: Yellow wood sorrels (Oxalis, same oxalate caveat)
species.safety_caveat: OXALATE caveat — moderation; avoid with kidney stones, gout, impaired kidney function. Also a contact caveat: do not confuse with poison oak (woody / oak-lobed).

species.edible_part: Leaves (young preferred); seeds also edible. Older leaves are fibrous.
species.preparation: Young leaves raw; older/larger leaves tough — cook like spinach.
species.season: Leaves spring–fall (young spring best); blooms Apr–Aug.
species.habitat: Roadsides, lawns, fields, trampled disturbed ground; basal rosette with dense narrow flower spikes.
species.range: Introduced, naturalized everywhere across PNW — WA, OR, S. BC, ID (HIGH, ubiquitous).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: First-year foxglove forms a ground-level rosette of soft veined leaves resembling a large plantain rosette; abundant in the same PNW roadsides/clearings.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Plantain leaves have 3–7 smooth PARALLEL veins base-to-tip, are hairless, with smooth/wavy edges; foxglove leaves are softly HAIRY (woolly underside) with a NETTED/pinnate vein pattern and finely toothed/scalloped margins. If in doubt, do not pick.

species.safety_caveat: The single most dangerous error is a young plantain rosette vs a first-year FOXGLOVE rosette (can be lethal). Confirm parallel venation and absence of soft hairs before handling. Avoid roadside/sprayed/dog-trafficked ground.

species.edible_part: Tender leaves, young stems, leafy shoot tips (peppery mustard flavor).
species.preparation: Best COOKED, not raw, from any wild/uncontrolled water — cooking destroys liver-fluke metacercariae and pathogens.
species.season: Aerial growth year-round in mild open Cascadia streams; flowers May–Sep; best before flowering.
species.habitat: Cold, slow-moving fresh water — spring seeps, stream/creek margins, irrigation ditches, ponds. SAME habitat as deadly water hemlock.
species.range: Naturalized, both sides of crest in WA; OR; S. BC; ID (HIGH at state level).

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Exact same habitat, often intermingled with watercress.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Water hemlock has white flowers in umbrella UMBELS (vs watercress's racemes of tiny 4-petaled flowers) and a thick CHAMBERED rootstock oozing yellowish oily liquid; watercress has fine fibrous node roots and no chambers.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Common in moist ditches/streamsides, can grow adjacent.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Poison hemlock has a hollow hairless stem with PURPLE blotches and a rank/mousy smell; watercress stems are fleshy, green, unspotted, rooting at nodes, smelling of peppery cress.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Apiaceae in the same cold streams; pinnate leaves resemble watercress when not flowering.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Umbels not racemes; a white ring/ridge around the leaf petiole base; more upright and sharply serrated.

benign_lookalikes: Fool's watercress (Apium nodiflorum) — an Apiaceae stepping-stone to a dangerous error
species.safety_caveat: DUAL CRITICAL WARNING. (1) DEADLY PROXIMITY: shares cold-stream/seep/ditch habitat with water hemlock and poison hemlock, often intermingled — never harvest without positively excluding every umbel-flowered carrot-family plant nearby. (2) RAW-WATER PARASITE: raw wild watercress is the classic vector for liver fluke (Fasciola) and can carry Giardia — cook thoroughly.

species.edible_part: Above-ground leaves, stems, flowers (like true miner's lettuce, C. perfoliata).
species.preparation: Raw OK (salad) or cooked. Cooking reduces soluble oxalate; moderation.
species.season: Tender greens in spring; blooms Mar–Sep.
species.habitat: Moist shady places — forest floors, streambanks, seeps, wet meadows; fleshy, opposite heart-shaped stem leaves, white-to-pink candy-striped flowers.
species.range: Native AK to CA, both sides of crest in WA, across S. BC. Idaho: MODERATE/LOW (SE margin of range only).
species.safety_caveat: Contains soluble OXALATES (relative C. perfoliata is documented high in oxalate); large/chronic consumption can stress kidneys. Moderation; avoid with kidney/oxalate issues; cook to reduce.

species.edible_part: Young PEELED leaf-stalks and flower-bud stalks, harvested in spring BEFORE flowering; dried seeds (spice). NOT the root, NOT mature/flowering stalks.
species.preparation: MUST PEEL + cooking recommended — unpeeled stalks cause itchy mouth/blistering. Handle with GLOVES; wash skin immediately after contact. Raw is not advised for non-experts.
species.season: Stalks/buds early Apr–late Jun before flowering; flowers Jun–Aug.
species.habitat: Moist areas — wet meadows, streambanks, roadsides, forest edges; robust single-stemmed perennial 1–3 m with broad maple-like lobed leaves.
species.range: WA both sides; OR; S. BC; AK to CA. Idaho: MEDIUM-HIGH.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Same family, tall stout stems, white compound umbels, coarse foliage, moist disturbed habitat.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Poison hemlock stems are hairless, smooth, with PURPLE blotches and finely ferny lacy foliage and a rank/mousy smell; cow parsnip stems are fuzzy/woolly-hairy, solid green (no purple), with broad maple-like rounded-lobed leaves.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Apiaceae, white umbels, overlapping wet habitats.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Water hemlock has narrow lance-shaped 2–3x pinnately divided feathery leaves and a chambered root; cow parsnip has broad maple-like leaflets and a non-chambered fuzzy stem.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Same genus, nearly identical gestalt — the most common dangerous confusion.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Hogweed is much larger (to ~14 ft) with conspicuously mottled purple/reddish bristly stems and deeply incised jagged leaflets; cow parsnip is smaller with solid-green soft-woolly stems and rounded leaf lobes. (Hogweed is a regulated invasive — report it.)

species.safety_caveat: TWO independent dangers. (1) PHOTOTOXIC SAP: furanocoumarins cause phytophotodermatitis (burning, blistering) with sun exposure — gloves, long sleeves, wash skin immediately. (2) DEADLY-LOOKALIKE RISK: routinely confused with poison hemlock and water hemlock (either can KILL with a small amount) and giant hogweed (severe burns). Never consume any wild Apiaceae without expert hands-on confirmation.

species.edible_part: Rhizomes (long fleshy white underground stems) — a documented traditional Indigenous root food.
species.preparation: Traditionally dug in fall, cleaned, and pit-cooked (steamed underground), often with Pacific silverweed; frequently dried for winter storage.
species.season: Harvested autumn; rhizomes stored for winter.
species.habitat: Coastal dunes, tidal/estuarine marshes, moist meadows, streambanks; stoloniferous perennial with reddish-purple flower heads.
species.range: BC south along the Pacific Coast, east to ID. WA chiefly west of the Cascades. Idaho: MODERATE-HIGH presence, but the food tradition is coastal.
benign_lookalikes: Wood sorrel (Oxalis) — sour, oxalate caution, not deadly; true clover has rounded/oval leaflets vs Oxalis heart-shaped notched
species.safety_caveat: A well-documented traditional Indigenous food of many Northwest Coast peoples. Confirm species ID; harvest sustainably (estuarine populations are limited).

species.edible_part: Bulb (deep-seated, tear-drop shaped). Historically a major NW Indigenous staple food.
species.preparation: Traditionally pit-roasted/steamed for an extended period (often 24–48+ hours), converting inulin to digestible fructose. Raw bulbs are not eaten. CRITICAL: cooking does NOT detoxify a misidentified death-camas bulb — preparation is irrelevant if species ID is wrong.
species.season: Flowering April–June (the ONLY reliable ID window).
species.habitat: Open moist meadows, prairies, grassy slopes that dry by late spring; lowland to mid-elevation.
species.range: S. BC to CA, WA/OR/ID, east to MT/UT/WY. HIGH at species level.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Grows INTERMIXED in the same meadows; bulbs and non-flowering grass-like leaves are essentially IDENTICAL — the single most dangerous confusion in this dataset.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: FLOWER COLOR is the only reliable field cue — camas = pale-to-deep BLUE/VIOLET; death camas = CREAM/WHITE to greenish. Bulbs are NOT diagnostic and neither has an onion odor. When not flowering, the two cannot be reliably separated.

species.safety_caveat: Positively identify ONLY when in flower and the flowers are unambiguously blue/violet. A single mixed-in white-flowered death-camas bulb can be fatal. When not flowering, do not harvest.

species.edible_part: Young shoots (inner core), rhizomes (starchy), pollen (flour extender), immature green flower spikes.
species.preparation: Shoots raw/cooked; immature spikes boiled/roasted; rhizomes baked/roasted or processed to starch; pollen as flour substitute.
species.season: Shoots spring; spikes late spring/early summer; pollen early–mid summer; rhizomes fall–early spring.
species.habitat: Marshes, ponds, lakeshores, slow water, wet ditches; sea level to mid-elevation.
species.range: Throughout North America; both sides of crest in WA. HIGH.

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Both form clumps of erect sword-like leaves in the same wetland margins; before flowering the rosettes look similar.
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: Cattail leaves are D-shaped/half-round (spongy), wrapping a rounded stalk, with no flat fan and no reddish base; iris leaves are flat in one plane with a raised midrib, in a flat basal fan, base often pink/red-tinged. The toxic iris rhizome is exactly the part most likely mistaken for the edible cattail rhizome.

species.safety_caveat: Chief hazard is confusing non-flowering cattail with yellow flag iris — vegetative-only ID is unreliable; never sample-taste to ID. Wetland foraging also carries pollutant/contamination risk in tubers/rhizomes.

species.edible_part: Starchy tubers/corms at the ends of underground rhizomes — NOT the leaves/stems/above-ground parts.
species.preparation: Cooked only — never raw (raw tubers are acrid). Boiled, roasted, or baked.
species.season: Flowers July–September; tubers traditionally harvested late summer–autumn into winter.
species.habitat: Wetlands — ditches, ponds, lake margins, marshes, shallow standing water and mud; emergent.
species.range: WA both sides (HIGH), OR (HIGH), S. BC (HIGH). Idaho: UNCONFIRMED (IDFG lists occurrence 'Unknown/Undetermined').

toxic_lookalikes.why_confused: Arrow-shaped (sagittate) leaf silhouette closely resembles Sagittaria; both emergent wetland plants. (Peltandra is mainly eastern, uncommon in the PNW.)
toxic_lookalikes.how_to_tell_apart: VENATION — Peltandra is pinnate-netted with a marginal collecting vein looping the edge; Sagittaria has several prominent near-parallel veins radiating from the base, no netted marginal vein. FLOWERS — Peltandra is an aroid spathe-and-spadix; Sagittaria has whorls of showy 3-petaled white flowers. Only Sagittaria makes edible terminal tubers.

benign_lookalikes: Other Sagittaria (e.g., S. cuneata) — congener confusion, not poisoning
species.safety_caveat: Dominant danger is misidentification by leaf shape. Calcium-oxalate aroids can cause severe oral/GI irritation and airway swelling. Confirm via flowers, venation, and the presence of true terminal tubers; when uncertain, do not consume.

toxic_reference.toxin: Ranunculin → protoanemonin (the buttercup toxin), most concentrated in roots/berries.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Burning mouth/throat, severe GI inflammation, vomiting, dizziness, irregular pulse. Poisonous, especially to children. Honesty flag: NO confirmed human fatalities from A. rubra in North America (documented fatalities are the European A. spicata); bitter taste limits intake. State 'poisonous — do not eat' without asserting a hard lethal dose.
toxic_reference.key_id: Soft herbaceous perennial (NOT woody), 40–80 cm; large 2–3-ternate sharply toothed maple-like compound leaves; white flowers in ONE dense terminal raceme; glossy smooth berries in ONE upright cluster, usually red (a white-fruited form exists — color not reliable); diagnostic: each berry has a dark dot/'eye' (stigma scar).
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Huckleberry is a woody shrub with dull berries borne singly along the branches and no eye; baneberry is a soft leafy herb with glossy berries in one upright cluster, each with a dark eye.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Glossy red (or white) berries in a single upright cluster atop a soft leafy plant, each with a dark eye = BANEBERRY.
toxic_reference.habitat: Moist shaded woods, streambanks, riparian; low to subalpine.
toxic_reference.range: Native and widespread across WA/OR/ID/S. BC.

toxic_reference.toxin: Cicutoxin (C17 polyacetylene), concentrated in roots/tubers (most toxic in early spring), present in all parts. One of the most violently toxic plants in North America.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: GABA-A antagonist: violent vomiting → grand mal seizures / status epilepticus → death by respiratory failure within ~15 min to a few hours. No antidote (supportive care + benzodiazepines). A small piece of root can kill an adult.
toxic_reference.key_id: Most reliable: CHAMBERED rootstock — sliced lengthwise the tuberous base shows horizontal internal chambers with cross-partitions, exuding yellowish oily liquid (edible carrot-family roots are solid, never chambered). Secondary: lateral leaf veins end in the notches between teeth, not at tooth tips (needs a hand lens, unreliable on young growth, never a 'safe' test). Leaves 1–3x pinnately compound, sharply serrate; stout hollow hairless stem; white compound umbels; 0.5–2 m.
confused_with: Pacific water parsley (Oenanthe sarmentosa, itself toxic)
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Never eat the root of any white-flowered wetland carrot-family plant. Elderberry is a woody shrub with opposite leaves and berries; water hemlock is herbaceous with alternate divided leaves and dry seeds. Purple stem mottling belongs to Conium (poison hemlock), NOT Cicuta.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: A sliced root with hollow horizontal chambers and oily yellow fluid = water hemlock; a thumbnail-sized piece can kill; no antidote.
toxic_reference.habitat: Obligate wetland — wet meadows, marshes, streambanks, ditches, pond margins, wet pasture.
toxic_reference.range: Native throughout WA/OR/ID/S. BC. (Allied C. maculata var. angustifolia east of the Cascades — treat as one deadly pair.)
toxic_reference.antidote: None; benzodiazepines for seizures + supportive care.

toxic_reference.toxin: Piperidine alkaloids — coniine and γ-coniceine (dominant in young plants). All parts toxic, including dried; seeds/roots most potent.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Curare-like neuromuscular block → ascending paralysis → respiratory arrest (the 'death of Socrates'), mind clear until late. A small amount can be lethal.
toxic_reference.key_id: Most reliable: smooth, completely hairless, hollow stem with purple/red/maroon blotches and streaks at all stages. Strong unpleasant musty/'mousy' odor when crushed (do not taste). Bright-green fern-like 3–4x pinnately divided leaves; white compound umbels; biennial, year-2 stalk often 6–10 ft.
confused_with: Wild carrot / Queen Anne's lace (Daucus carota)
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Queen Anne's lace has a HAIRY stem, no purple blotches, is 1–3 ft, and smells of carrot. Poison hemlock has a hairless purple-blotched stem and a musty smell. No safe taste test. (Distinct from water hemlock: coniine/paralysis vs cicutoxin/seizures.)
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: A lacy white-flowered 'wild carrot' with a smooth hairless stem bearing purple blotches/streaks = POISON HEMLOCK.
toxic_reference.habitat: Introduced; common and widespread — roadsides, ditches, canals, fence lines, waste/disturbed ground, streambanks; rich moist soil.
toxic_reference.range: Class B noxious weed in WA and OR; regulated in ID/BC. Widespread in the PNW.

toxic_reference.toxin: Steroidal alkaloids (chiefly zygacine) throughout bulb/foliage/pollen/nectar/seed. Toxins survive cooking and drying — cooking does NOT detoxify.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Holds sodium channels open → hypotension, bradycardia, cardiovascular collapse, GI + neuromuscular effects; symptoms 1–8 hr, can progress to convulsions, coma, death. No antidote. A small number of bulbs can be fatal. A 2024 case poisoned 7 family members who foraged it as wild onions.
toxic_reference.key_id: Most reliable: flower color creamy/greenish-white — NEVER blue/purple. Bulb resembles an onion but has NO onion/garlic odor. Small 6-tepaled flowers in a raceme/panicle, blooms April–June; basal grass-like V-keeled linear leaves.
confused_with: Blue camas (Camassia quamash, C. leichtlinii) — edible
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Edible camas has BLUE/blue-purple flowers; death camas has creamy/white flowers — and their bulbs are essentially identical out of flower, so harvest blue camas ONLY in bloom and discard anything not clearly blue. The 'no onion smell' bulb test is a SECONDARY check, not foolproof (leaves can smell oniony; handling real onions can mask it) — flower color is the reliable rule.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Never harvest camas bulbs out of flower; harvest only in bloom and discard every plant whose flowers are not clearly BLUE/PURPLE. A grass-like 'wild onion' bulb with no onion smell may be deadly death camas.
toxic_reference.habitat: Open sunny meadows, prairies, grasslands, balds, shrub-steppe, ponderosa openings; frequently grows alongside blue camas.
toxic_reference.antidote: None; supportive care (atropine for bradycardia, pressors for hypotension).

toxic_reference.toxin: Cardiac (cardenolide) glycosides — chiefly digitoxin. All parts toxic at every stage, including the first-year rosette.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Inhibit Na+/K+-ATPase → arrhythmias, bradycardia, AV/heart block, hyperkalemia; death from ventricular fibrillation/asystole. Signs: vomiting, weakness, visual disturbance (yellow-green halos). Small leaf amounts (e.g., as 'tea') have killed. A hospital antidote exists (digoxin-specific Fab / DigiFab).
toxic_reference.key_id: Second-year (diagnostic): tall unbranched ONE-SIDED raceme of large tubular/bell-shaped pendulous pink-purple (sometimes white) flowers with a conspicuously SPOTTED throat; blooms May–Aug. First-year rosette (the confusion stage): soft, downy, gray-green lance/egg-shaped leaves, finely toothed/scalloped, with prominent quilted impressed veins and a hairy whitish underside. Biennial, 2–6 ft in flower.
confused_with: Comfrey (Symphytum officinale) — the classic deadly swap
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Foxglove leaves are finely TOOTHED/scalloped; comfrey leaves are smooth/untoothed (entire) with coarse bristly hairs and leaves running down the stem. The year-1 rosette has no flowers — exactly when it is confused with comfrey — yet is loaded with lethal cardiac glycosides.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Never gather, brew, or eat foxglove leaves as comfrey (or borage/greens). Reliable distinction: foxglove leaves are toothed/scalloped; comfrey leaves are smooth-edged.
toxic_reference.habitat: Introduced, widely naturalized/invasive — roadsides, logged/clearcut areas, forest edges/clearings, fields, disturbed ground.
toxic_reference.range: Abundant in WA & OR chiefly west of the Cascade crest and coastal/SW BC. In ID present but scattered/localized (confidence MODERATE).
toxic_reference.antidote: Digoxin-specific antibody fragments (DigiFab) in hospital; supportive care.

toxic_reference.toxin: Steroidal Veratrum alkaloids (veratridine, jervine, veratramine, cyclopamine) — cardiotoxic (hypotension + bradycardia). Whole plant including young shoots; roots/rhizomes most toxic.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Severe poisoning from even a small amount (documented onset after a single leaf). Foragers are poisoned by mistaking young emerging shoots for edible greens. No specific antidote.
toxic_reference.key_id: Tall (1–2 m); large broad strongly pleated/ribbed leaves with prominent parallel veins (corn-like), bases clasping an erect leafy stem, alternate up the stalk; yellow-green flowers in branched clusters.
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: vs skunk cabbage — Veratrum has pleated parallel-veined leaves clasping a leafy erect stem; skunk cabbage has broadly oval netted-vein leaves arising only from the base. vs ramps/Allium — Allium smells of onion/garlic; Veratrum has no allium odor.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: If a 'wild green' has pleated, parallel-veined leaves clasping an upright stalk and no onion smell, it is false hellebore — do not eat it.
toxic_reference.habitat: Moist meadows, streambanks, seeps, moist woodlands; lowlands to subalpine.
toxic_reference.range: AK to CA, east to N. Rockies; widespread in WA. WA/OR/ID/BC HIGH.
toxic_reference.antidote: No specific antidote — supportive care; atropine for symptomatic bradycardia; vasopressors for hypotension.

toxic_reference.toxin: Calcium oxalate raphides (needle crystals) throughout the plant.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Eaten raw, raphides cause intense burning of the mouth/throat/esophagus, oral/pharyngeal irritation, possible throat swelling, and GI irritation. Airway swelling is the serious concern.
toxic_reference.key_id: Early-spring large bright-yellow hooded spathe (~12–20 in) around a fleshy spike; very large broadly elliptical basal leaves in a rosette; skunky odor (mild in the western species — do not over-rely on smell).
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Skunk cabbage has broadly oval NETTED-vein leaves from the base only, a bright-yellow spathe, and a skunky smell; Veratrum has pleated PARALLEL-veined leaves clasping an upright leafy stalk.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: A wetland plant with a bright-yellow hood (spathe), skunky odor, and big basal netted-vein leaves is skunk cabbage — toxic raw, not a salad green.
toxic_reference.habitat: Swamps, marshes, forested wetlands, bog margins, seeps; sea level to mid-elevation.
toxic_reference.range: SE Alaska through BC into NW California; abundant west of the Cascades. HIGH for WA/OR/coastal S. BC; lower for N. Idaho margins.
toxic_reference.antidote: No specific antidote — rinse mouth, cool fluids/demulcents, monitor for airway swelling; emergency care if breathing difficulty.

toxic_reference.toxin: Steroidal glycoalkaloids — solanine (highest in unripe green berries + foliage), solasodine, dulcamarine. Ripe red berries are less toxic but still not safe.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: GI effects (nausea, vomiting, pain, diarrhea) plus neurological effects (dizziness, drowsiness, confusion, dilated pupils). The bright red berries attract young children — the primary at-risk group.
toxic_reference.key_id: Trailing/climbing vine to ~10 ft; star-shaped purple/violet flowers with a forward-pointing yellow cone of fused stamens; egg-shaped berries ripening green → orange → glossy red in drooping clusters; dark arrowhead leaves with small basal lobes; foul smell when crushed.
confused_with: Not a major edible mimic — risk is that shiny red berries look candy-like to children
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: The purple flower with a protruding yellow 'beak' and drooping red berry clusters on a woody vine match no common edible.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Do not let children pick the shiny red berries — they are toxic, not a wild edible.
toxic_reference.habitat: Moist disturbed sites, streambanks, wetland edges, hedgerows, gardens, roadsides, shaded thickets.
toxic_reference.range: Introduced (Eurasian); widespread and common throughout the PNW.
toxic_reference.antidote: No specific antidote — supportive care; poison control 1-800-222-1222.

toxic_reference.toxin: Tropane alkaloids — atropine, scopolamine, hyoscyamine — throughout the plant (roots most concentrated).
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Potent anticholinergic — dilated pupils, dry flushed skin, tachycardia, fever, CNS confusion/hallucinations/delirium/seizures; severe cases coma/respiratory failure/death. The sweet shiny black berries are especially dangerous to children — a small number can be fatal.
toxic_reference.key_id: Tall bushy perennial; drooping bell-shaped flowers dull reddish/brownish-purple; SINGLE shiny black berries (not tight clusters) seated in a persistent green 5-pointed star calyx; ovate leaves in unequal pairs.
confused_with: Black berries mistaken for blueberries/huckleberries — primary lethal risk, especially for children
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Belladonna berries are borne singly (not in grape-like clusters or on blueberry shrubs) in a green star calyx, on a tall bushy herb with brownish-purple bell flowers.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Never mistake the single shiny black berries for blueberries — a small amount can be fatal to a child.
toxic_reference.habitat: Disturbed ground, dumps, quarries, waste sites, roadsides, shaded calcareous soils; mostly a garden/medicinal escape.
toxic_reference.range: FLAG — uncommon/rare in Cascadia (scattered naturalized escapes). A reported 'deadly nightshade' here is statistically far more likely to be misidentified bittersweet nightshade.
toxic_reference.antidote: Physostigmine for severe anticholinergic toxicity (used cautiously); otherwise supportive — activated charcoal, benzodiazepines, ICU monitoring. Poison control 1-800-222-1222.

toxic_reference.toxin: Uncertain/poorly characterized — reported saponins, possibly isoquinoline alkaloids and/or calcium oxalate. Toxin attribution is LOW confidence; 'do not eat' is HIGH confidence.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: White berries are mildly toxic — nausea, vomiting, dizziness, especially in quantity; a concern for children drawn to the conspicuous white winter-persistent berries.
toxic_reference.key_id: Native deciduous shrub (~1–2 m) with wiry arching branches; round waxy SNOW-WHITE berries 8–12 mm in clusters persisting into winter; small pinkish bell flowers; small opposite oval leaves.
confused_with: No good edible white-berry lookalike — there are essentially no common edible pure-white PNW berries; the risk is curiosity-eating
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Opaque waxy pure-white round berries on a wiry native shrub with paired leaves and pinkish bell flowers are diagnostic.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Pure-white berries are not a wild edible — snowberries cause stomach upset and should never be eaten.
toxic_reference.habitat: Woodlands, forest edges, streambanks, thickets; low to mid elevation.
toxic_reference.range: PNW native and very common — SE Alaska to S. California, east to the Rockies; widespread WA/OR/ID/BC both sides of the crest.
toxic_reference.antidote: No specific antidote — supportive care for GI upset; poison control if a child ingests.

toxic_reference.toxin: Irritant glycosides + resinous compounds, highest in the rhizomes.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: GI gastroenteritis (nausea, vomiting, pain, diarrhea); sap/resins cause contact dermatitis (agencies advise gloves/long sleeves). Effects are irritant/purgative rather than typically fatal (human lethal data not well established — qualitative).
toxic_reference.key_id: Stiff erect sword-shaped flat leaves (blue-green) with a raised midrib, overlapping in a flat basal fan; showy yellow flowers (May–July); 3-/6-angled seed capsules with corky floating seeds; thick fleshy mat-forming rhizomes.
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: vs cattail — iris leaf is flat in one plane with a midrib and a flat basal fan, often pink/red-tinged at base; cattail leaf is thicker, D-shaped/spongy, wraps a round stalk, with no fan and no reddish base. vs Acorus — Acorus is strongly aromatic when crushed; iris is not.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Before it flowers, the iris's sword-leaf fan mimics non-flowering cattail — never ID cattail by leaves alone, because the toxic iris rhizome is exactly the part most likely mistaken for the edible cattail rhizome.
toxic_reference.habitat: Wetlands, lake/pond margins, irrigation ditches, slow streams, marsh/mud.
toxic_reference.range: Eurasian; introduced and invasive in the PNW; WA both sides (more common west), BC to CA, east to ID/MT. Washington Class C noxious weed.
toxic_reference.antidote: No specific antidote — supportive/symptomatic; wash skin; poison control 1-800-222-1222.

synonyms: Delphinium spp. (genus-level; also D. occidentale, low larkspurs)
toxic_reference.toxin: Norditerpenoid alkaloids — highly toxic MSAL-type (methyllycaconitine) and MDL-type. Mechanism: competitive blockade of nicotinic ACh receptors → neuromuscular paralysis.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Leading plant cause of cattle death on western US rangelands; also toxic to humans (rare but documented, can be fatal — GI irritation, neuromuscular blockade, cardiotoxicity). All parts toxic; new growth and seeds carry the highest alkaloid load.
toxic_reference.key_id: Tall (1–2 m) stout glabrous glaucous perennial with hollow stems; palmately divided leaves; an elongate raceme of deep blue-purple flowers; the upper sepal has a backward-projecting SPUR.
confused_with: young palmate leaves vs other broad-leaved forbs
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Larkspur flower has a SPUR; monkshood has a HOOD/helmet (no spur). When not flowering, larkspur stems are hollow. Leaf-only ID is unreliable.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: The spurred blue flower (larkspur) and the hooded blue flower (monkshood) are distinct but BOTH deadly — never treat either as safe.
toxic_reference.habitat: Mountain meadows, wet thickets, bogs, streambanks, conifer-forest openings; middle elevations.
toxic_reference.range: Native PNW mountains; D. glaucum both sides of the crest in WA; AK to CA, east to the Rockies — WA, OR, ID, S. BC.
toxic_reference.antidote: No specific human antidote — supportive/respiratory care.

toxic_reference.toxin: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (jacobine, senecionine) — bioactivated in the liver to reactive pyrroles → cumulative, irreversible liver damage.
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Repeated small doses cause fatal liver failure (cumulative — there is no recovery threshold). PAs contaminate the food chain — documented in honey and milk (a human exposure route). All parts toxic; highest in flowers.
toxic_reference.key_id: Biennial; first-year rosette of dark green ruffled basal leaves; second-year stems to ~4 ft; flat-topped clusters of yellow daisy-like heads (typically 13 ray florets); twice-divided deeply lobed ragged leaves.
confused_with: Common tansy (Tanacetum vulgare) — the classic name swap
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: Common tansy has button-like RAYLESS yellow heads; ragwort has obvious ray florets ('petals'). St. John's-wort has 5 petals (not a composite).
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Tansy ragwort's daisy-like RAYED yellow heads are not common tansy's rayless yellow buttons — the names are swapped constantly; ragwort is the cumulative liver poison (common tansy is also toxic).
toxic_reference.habitat: Open disturbed ground — roadsides, pastures, fields, cleared/logged land.
toxic_reference.range: Introduced Eurasian invasive; widespread PNW, most common west of the Cascades; present in eastern WA and S. BC. Washington Class B noxious weed.
toxic_reference.antidote: None — liver damage is irreversible/cumulative; supportive care only; prognosis poor once clinical signs appear.

toxic_reference.toxin: Cardiac glycosides (chiefly convallatoxin) + saponins — inhibit myocardial Na+/K+-ATPase (same class as digoxin/foxglove/oleander).
toxic_reference.why_dangerous: Cardiotoxic — nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, then bradycardia/arrhythmias, potential cardiac arrest. All parts poisonous including leaves, flowers, roots, and the red-orange berries; toxins even leach into cut-flower vase water. (Serious human poisoning is somewhat uncommon because the glycosides are poorly absorbed orally, but ingestion is a medical emergency.)
toxic_reference.key_id: Low perennial (~18–30 cm); typically two paired broad elliptical leaves with parallel/arching veins; an arching one-sided raceme of small white nodding fragrant bell flowers; a red-orange berry (6–12 mm) in late summer.
confused_with: Wild garlic / ramps (Allium ursinum) and wild onion greens before flowering — a documented serious/fatal poisoning vector
toxic_reference.how_to_tell_apart: SMELL — wild garlic/ramps smell strongly of onion/garlic when crushed; lily-of-the-valley has NO onion/garlic odor. Allium leaves are on individual stalks; lily-of-the-valley leaves emerge paired from a basal sheath.
toxic_reference.do_not_confuse: Lily-of-the-valley's odorless paired leaves are easily mistaken for onion-smelling wild garlic/ramps, and eating them can be fatal — if the crushed leaf has no onion/garlic smell, never eat it.
toxic_reference.habitat: Shaded moist woodland and garden beds; commonly a garden ornamental/escape.
toxic_reference.range: FLAG — primarily cultivated / a garden escape in the PNW, not a widespread naturalized wild plant; encounters most likely in/near gardens and landscaping.
toxic_reference.antidote: Supportive cardiac care with ECG monitoring and electrolyte correction; digoxin-specific antibody fragments (DigiFab) for severe cardiac-glycoside poisoning. Poison control 1-800-222-1222.
